2026-04-09T20:58:07.6888942Z Now I have enough information to file the issue. The PR #57905 introduced a major architectural change: Azure.Identity types were consolidated into Azure.Core with TypeForwardedTo attributes, making Azure.Identity a lightweight facade. The README.md doesn't reflect this change.

2026-04-09T21:01:44.1969828Z Now I'll create the issue. The documentation gap is clear: the Azure.Identity README doesn't explain that as of version 1.53.0-beta.1, all Azure.Identity types now live in Azure.Core (types are forwarded via TypeForwardedTo attributes), making Azure.Identity a lightweight facade. The MigrationGuide.md exists but is not referenced from the README.

2026-04-09T21:06:48.1177448Z {"create_issue": {"repo": "Azure/azure-sdk-for-net", "title": "[Azure Identity] Docs: README doesn't reflect type consolidation into Azure.Core (1.53.0)", "body": "## Documentation Gap\n\n**Package:** `Azure.Identity`\n**Service directory:** `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/`\n**Triggered by:** commit ad66d9799 / PR #57905 by @JonathanCrd\n\n## What Changed\n\nPR #57905 completed the consolidation of all `Azure.Identity` credential types into `Azure.Core`. A new `TypeForwarders.cs` file was added to `Azure.Identity` that type-forwards all credential types (e.g. `DefaultAzureCredential`, `ClientSecretCredential`, `ManagedIdentityCredential`, etc.) to their new home in `Azure.Core`. The CHANGELOG for `1.53.0-beta.1` notes: _\"All `Azure.Identity` types have been moved to `Azure.Core` and are now available through `TypeForwardedTo` attributes.\"_\n\nA `MigrationGuide.md` was also added covering CS0433 upgrade scenarios — but it is **not linked from the README**.\n\n## Gaps Found\n\n- **README intro paragraph is stale**: says Azure.Identity \"provides a set of `TokenCredential` implementations\" — these implementations now live in `Azure.Core`, not in `Azure.Identity`. The package description should reflect that it is now a lightweight type-forwarding facade.\n- **README header missing migration guide link**: the canonical README pattern (e.g., `sdk/keyvault/Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets/README.md`) includes a `[Migration guide][migration_guide]` link in the header. `MigrationGuide.md` exists but is not referenced anywhere in the README.\n- **\"Install the package\" section doesn't mention the new availability**: users who only depend on `Azure.Core` 1.53.0+ can now use all credential types (`DefaultAzureCredential`, etc.) without adding an `Azure.Identity` reference at all. This should be documented.\n- **\"Key concepts → Credentials\" section is inaccurate**: says \"Each credential in this library is an implementation of the `TokenCredential` abstract class in Azure.Core\" — credentials now compile into `Azure.Core` directly; Azure.Identity is merely a facade.\n- **No \"Next steps\" link to MigrationGuide.md**: users upgrading from `Azure.Identity` < 1.53.0 may encounter CS0433 errors; the migration guide addresses this but is invisible to README readers.\n\n<details>\n<summary><strong>📐 Implementation Guide</strong></summary>\n\nThis section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above.\n\n### Step 1: Modify files\n\n#### File: `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md` — Edit\n\n**1a. Update the intro paragraph and add migration guide header link** (lines 3–5)\n\nReplace:\n```markdown\nThe Azure Identity library provides [Microsoft Entra ID](https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/fundamentals/whatis) token-based authentication support across the Azure SDK. It provides a set of [`TokenCredential`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/azure.core.tokencredential?view=azure-dotnet) implementations that can be used to construct Azure SDK clients that support Microsoft Entra token authentication.\n\n[Source code][source] | [Package (NuGet)][package] | [API reference documentation][identity_api_docs] | [Microsoft Entra ID documentation][entraid_doc]\n```\n\nWith:\n```markdown\nThe Azure Identity library provides [Microsoft Entra ID](https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/fundamentals/whatis) token-based authentication support across the Azure SDK. Starting with version 1.53.0, all credential types (e.g. `DefaultAzureCredential`, `ClientSecretCredential`, `ManagedIdentityCredential`) are compiled into `Azure.Core` and `Azure.Identity` acts as a lightweight type-forwarding facade. Existing code continues to work without changes.\n\n[Source code][source] | [Package (NuGet)][package] | [API reference documentation][identity_api_docs] | [Microsoft Entra ID documentation][entraid_doc] | [Migration guide][migration_guide]\n```\n\n**1b. Update the \"Install the package\" section** — add a note after the closing fence of the `dotnet add package Azure.Identity` block:\n\n```markdown\n> **Note:** As of `Azure.Core` 1.53.0, all credential types are built into `Azure.Core`. If your project already depends on `Azure.Core` 1.53.0 or later, you can use `DefaultAzureCredential` and all other credential types **without** adding a direct `Azure.Identity` reference. See the [Migration Guide][migration_guide] if you encounter `CS0433` compiler errors when upgrading.\n```\n\n**1c. Update the \"Key concepts → Credentials\" section** (around line 32)\n\nReplace:\n```\nThe Azure Identity library focuses on OAuth authentication with Microsoft Entra ID. It offers numerous credentials capable of acquiring a Microsoft Entra token to authenticate service requests. Each credential in this library is an implementation of the `TokenCredential` abstract class in [Azure.Core][azure_core_library], and any of them can be used to construct service clients capable of authenticating with a `TokenCredential`.\n```\n\nWith:\n```\nThe Azure Identity library focuses on OAuth authentication with Microsoft Entra ID. It offers numerous credentials capable of acquiring a Microsoft Entra token to authenticate service requests. Each credential is an implementation of the `TokenCredential` abstract class in [Azure.Core][azure_core_library]. Starting with `Azure.Core` 1.53.0, all credential types are compiled directly into `Azure.Core` — `Azure.Identity` is a lightweight facade that type-forwards all types for backward compatibility.\n```\n\n**1d. Add a \"Migration\" subsection under \"Next steps\"** (after the \"Known issues\" subsection)\n\nAdd:\n```markdown\n### Upgrading to 1.53.0\n\nAll credential types are now available directly from `Azure.Core` 1.53.0. See the [Migration Guide][migration_guide] for:\n- How to remove or update a direct `Azure.Identity` reference\n- How to fix `CS0433` errors caused by duplicate type definitions when upgrading\n```\n\n**1e. Add the `[migration_guide]` link reference** at the bottom of the README in the `<!-- LINKS -->` section:\n\n```markdown\n[migration_guide]: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/MigrationGuide.md\n```\n\n### Step 2: Add or update code snippets\n\nNo new snippet is required for the README changes described above. The existing `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/tests/samples/ReadmeSnippets.cs` snippets remain valid and unchanged.\n\n### Step 3: Verify README structure\n\nThe README at `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md` already contains all required sections in order:\n1. ✅ Getting started (Install the package, Prerequisites, Authenticate the client)\n2. ✅ Key concepts\n3. ✅ Examples\n4. ✅ Troubleshooting\n5. ✅ Next steps\n6. ✅ Contributing\n\nNo sections are missing; only the content within existing sections needs updating as described in Step 1.\n\n### Step 4: Validate\n\nRun these commands in order. Each must succeed before proceeding to the next.\n\n1. `dotnet build sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/`\n2. `dotnet test sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/ --filter TestCategory!=Live`\n3. `eng/scripts/Update-Snippets.ps1 identity` — confirm README snippet blocks are still injected correctly after edits; check git diff to verify\n4. `eng/scripts/Export-API.ps1 identity` — no public API changed, but run to confirm no regressions\n5. `dotnet format sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/`\n\n</details>\n\n## Next Steps\n\n> [!TIP]\n> **Ready for automated implementation?** Assign this issue to **@copilot** to have Copilot coding agent implement the changes described in the Implementation Guide above\n"}}

2026-04-09T21:07:12.3036191Z {"create_issue": {"repo": "Azure/azure-sdk-for-net", "title": "[Azure Identity] Docs: README doesn't reflect type consolidation into Azure.Core (1.53.0)", "body": "## Documentation Gap\n\n**Package:** `Azure.Identity`\n**Service directory:** `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/`\n**Triggered by:** commit ad66d9799 / PR #57905 by @JonathanCrd\n\n## What Changed\n\nPR #57905 completed the consolidation of all `Azure.Identity` credential types into `Azure.Core`. A new `TypeForwarders.cs` file was added to `Azure.Identity` that type-forwards all credential types (e.g. `DefaultAzureCredential`, `ClientSecretCredential`, `ManagedIdentityCredential`, etc.) to their new home in `Azure.Core`. The CHANGELOG for `1.53.0-beta.1` notes: _\"All `Azure.Identity` types have been moved to `Azure.Core` and are now available through `TypeForwardedTo` attributes.\"_\n\nA `MigrationGuide.md` was also added covering CS0433 upgrade scenarios — but it is **not linked from the README**.\n\n## Gaps Found\n\n- **README intro paragraph is stale**: says Azure.Identity \"provides a set of `TokenCredential` implementations\" — these implementations now live in `Azure.Core`, not in `Azure.Identity`. The package description should reflect that it is now a lightweight type-forwarding facade.\n- **README header missing migration guide link**: the canonical README pattern (e.g., `sdk/keyvault/Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets/README.md`) includes a `[Migration guide][migration_guide]` link in the header. `MigrationGuide.md` exists but is not referenced anywhere in the README.\n- **\"Install the package\" section doesn't mention the new availability**: users who only depend on `Azure.Core` 1.53.0+ can now use all credential types without adding an `Azure.Identity` reference. This should be documented.\n- **\"Key concepts → Credentials\" section is inaccurate**: says \"Each credential in this library is an implementation of the `TokenCredential` abstract class in Azure.Core\" — credentials now compile into `Azure.Core` directly; Azure.Identity is merely a facade.\n- **No \"Next steps\" link to MigrationGuide.md**: users upgrading from `Azure.Identity` < 1.53.0 may encounter CS0433 errors; the migration guide addresses this but is invisible to README readers.\n\n<details>\n<summary><strong>📐 Implementation Guide</strong></summary>\n\nThis section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above.\n\n### Step 1: Modify files\n\n#### File: `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md` — Edit\n\n**1a. Update the intro paragraph and add migration guide header link** (lines 3–5)\n\nReplace:\n\n```markdown\nThe Azure Identity library provides [Microsoft Entra ID](https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/fundamentals/whatis) token-based authentication support across the Azure SDK. It provides a set of [`TokenCredential`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/azure.core.tokencredential?view=azure-dotnet) implementations that can be used to construct Azure SDK clients that support Microsoft Entra token authentication.\n\n[Source code][source] | [Package (NuGet)][package] | [API reference documentation][identity_api_docs] | [Microsoft Entra ID documentation][entraid_doc]\n```\n\nWith:\n\n```markdown\nThe Azure Identity library provides [Microsoft Entra ID](https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/fundamentals/whatis) token-based authentication support across the Azure SDK. Starting with version 1.53.0, all credential types (e.g. `DefaultAzureCredential`, `ClientSecretCredential`, `ManagedIdentityCredential`) are compiled into `Azure.Core` and `Azure.Identity` acts as a lightweight type-forwarding facade. Existing code continues to work without changes.\n\n[Source code][source] | [Package (NuGet)][package] | [API reference documentation][identity_api_docs] | [Microsoft Entra ID documentation][entraid_doc] | [Migration guide][migration_guide]\n```\n\n**1b. Update the \"Install the package\" section** — add a note after the closing fence of the `dotnet add package Azure.Identity` block:\n\n```markdown\n> **Note:** As of `Azure.Core` 1.53.0, all credential types are built into `Azure.Core`. If your project already depends on `Azure.Core` 1.53.0 or later, you can use `DefaultAzureCredential` and all other credential types **without** adding a direct `Azure.Identity` reference. See the [Migration Guide][migration_guide] if you encounter `CS0433` compiler errors when upgrading.\n```\n\n**1c. Update the \"Key concepts → Credentials\" section** (around line 32)\n\nReplace:\n\n```\nThe Azure Identity library focuses on OAuth authentication with Microsoft Entra ID. It offers numerous credentials capable of acquiring a Microsoft Entra token to authenticate service requests. Each credential in this library is an implementation of the `TokenCredential` abstract class in [Azure.Core][azure_core_library], and any of them can be used to construct service clients capable of authenticating with a `TokenCredential`.\n```\n\nWith:\n\n```\nThe Azure Identity library focuses on OAuth authentication with Microsoft Entra ID. It offers numerous credentials capable of acquiring a Microsoft Entra token to authenticate service requests. Each credential is an implementation of the `TokenCredential` abstract class in [Azure.Core][azure_core_library]. Starting with `Azure.Core` 1.53.0, all credential types are compiled directly into `Azure.Core` — `Azure.Identity` is a lightweight facade that type-forwards all types for backward compatibility.\n```\n\n**1d. Add a \"Upgrading to 1.53.0\" subsection under \"Next steps\"** (after the \"Known issues\" subsection)\n\nAdd:\n\n```markdown\n### Upgrading to 1.53.0\n\nAll credential types are now available directly from `Azure.Core` 1.53.0. See the [Migration Guide][migration_guide] for:\n- How to remove or update a direct `Azure.Identity` reference\n- How to fix `CS0433` errors caused by duplicate type definitions when upgrading\n```\n\n**1e. Add the `[migration_guide]` link reference** at the bottom of the README in the `<!-- LINKS -->` section:\n\n```markdown\n[migration_guide]: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/MigrationGuide.md\n```\n\n### Step 2: Add or update code snippets\n\nNo new snippet is required for the README changes described above. The existing `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/tests/samples/ReadmeSnippets.cs` snippets remain valid and unchanged.\n\n### Step 3: Verify README structure\n\nThe README at `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md` already contains all required sections in order:\n1. Getting started (Install the package, Prerequisites, Authenticate the client)\n2. Key concepts\n3. Examples\n4. Troubleshooting\n5. Next steps\n6. Contributing\n\nNo sections are missing; only the content within existing sections needs updating as described in Step 1.\n\n### Step 4: Validate\n\nRun these commands in order. Each must succeed before proceeding to the next. 2026-04-09T21:08:20.3882222Z {"create_issue": {"repo": "Azure/azure-sdk-for-net", "title": "[Azure Identity] Docs: README doesn't reflect type consolidation into Azure.Core (1.53.0)", "body": "## Documentation Gap\n\n**Package:** `Azure.Identity`\n**Service directory:** `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/`\n**Triggered by:** commit ad66d9799 / PR #57905 by @JonathanCrd\n\n## What Changed\n\nPR #57905 completed the consolidation of all `Azure.Identity` credential types into `Azure.Core`. A new `TypeForwarders.cs` file was added to `Azure.Identity` that type-forwards all credential types (e.g. `DefaultAzureCredential`, `ClientSecretCredential`, `ManagedIdentityCredential`, etc.) to their new home in `Azure.Core`. The CHANGELOG for `1.53.0-beta.1` notes: _\"All `Azure.Identity` types have been moved to `Azure.Core` and are now available through `TypeForwardedTo` attributes.\"_\n\nA `MigrationGuide.md` was also added covering CS0433 upgrade scenarios — but it is **not linked from the README**.\n\n## Gaps Found\n\n- **README intro paragraph is stale**: says Azure.Identity \"provides a set of `TokenCredential` implementations\" — these implementations now live in `Azure.Core`, not in `Azure.Identity`. The package description should reflect that it is now a lightweight type-forwarding facade.\n- **README header missing migration guide link**: the canonical README pattern (e.g., `sdk/keyvault/Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets/README.md`) includes a `[Migration guide][migration_guide]` link in the header. `MigrationGuide.md` exists but is not referenced anywhere in the README.\n- **\"Install the package\" section doesn't mention the new availability**: users who only depend on `Azure.Core` 1.53.0+ can now use all credential types without adding an `Azure.Identity` reference. This should be documented.\n- **\"Key concepts Credentials\" section is inaccurate**: says \"Each credential in this library is an implementation of the `TokenCredential` abstract class in Azure.Core\" — credentials now compile into `Azure.Core` directly; Azure.Identity is merely a facade.\n- **No \"Next steps\" link to MigrationGuide.md**: users upgrading from `Azure.Identity` < 1.53.0 may encounter CS0433 errors; the migration guide addresses this but is invisible to README readers.\n\n<details>\n<summary><strong>📐 Implementation Guide</strong></summary>\n\nThis section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above.\n\n### Step 1: Modify files\n\n#### File: `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md` — Edit\n\n**1a. Update the intro paragraph and add migration guide header link** (lines 3-5)\n\nReplace:\n\n```markdown\nThe Azure Identity library provides [Microsoft Entra ID](https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/fundamentals/whatis) token-based authentication support across the Azure SDK. It provides a set of [`TokenCredential`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/azure.core.tokencredential?view=azure-dotnet) implementations that can be used to construct Azure SDK clients that support Microsoft Entra token authentication.\n\n[Source code][source] | [Package (NuGet)][package] | [API reference documentation][identity_api_docs] | [Microsoft Entra ID documentation][entraid_doc]\n```\n\nWith:\n\n```markdown\nThe Azure Identity library provides [Microsoft Entra ID](https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/fundamentals/whatis) token-based authentication support across the Azure SDK. Starting with version 1.53.0, all credential types (e.g. `DefaultAzureCredential`, `ClientSecretCredential`, `ManagedIdentityCredential`) are compiled into `Azure.Core` and `Azure.Identity` acts as a lightweight type-forwarding facade. Existing code continues to work without changes.\n\n[Source code][source] | [Package (NuGet)][package] | [API reference documentation][identity_api_docs] | [Microsoft Entra ID documentation][entraid_doc] | [Migration guide][migration_guide]\n```\n\n**1b. Update the \"Install the package\" section** — add a note after the closing fence of the `dotnet add package Azure.Identity` block:\n\n```markdown\n> **Note:** As of `Azure.Core` 1.53.0, all credential types are built into `Azure.Core`. If your project already depends on `Azure.Core` 1.53.0 or later, you can use `DefaultAzureCredential` and all other credential types **without** adding a direct `Azure.Identity` reference. See the [Migration Guide][migration_guide] if you encounter `CS0433` compiler errors when upgrading.\n```\n\n**1c. Update the \"Key concepts - Credentials\" section** (around line 32)\n\nReplace:\n\n```\nThe Azure Identity library focuses on OAuth authentication with Microsoft Entra ID. It offers numerous credentials capable of acquiring a Microsoft Entra token to authenticate service requests. Each credential in this library is an implementation of the `TokenCredential` abstract class in [Azure.Core][azure_core_library], and any of them can be used to construct service clients capable of authenticating with a `TokenCredential`.\n```\n\nWith:\n\n```\nThe Azure Identity library focuses on OAuth authentication with Microsoft Entra ID. It offers numerous credentials capable of acquiring a Microsoft Entra token to authenticate service requests. Each credential is an implementation of the `TokenCredential` abstract class in [Azure.Core][azure_core_library]. Starting with `Azure.Core` 1.53.0, all credential types are compiled directly into `Azure.Core` — `Azure.Identity` is a lightweight facade that type-forwards all types for backward compatibility.\n```\n\n**1d. Add an \"Upgrading to 1.53.0\" subsection under \"Next steps\"** (after the \"Known issues\" subsection)\n\nAdd:\n\n```markdown\n### Upgrading to 1.53.0\n\nAll credential types are now available directly from `Azure.Core` 1.53.0. See the [Migration Guide][migration_guide] for:\n- How to remove or update a direct `Azure.Identity` reference\n- How to fix `CS0433` errors caused by duplicate type definitions when upgrading\n```\n\n**1e. Add the `[migration_guide]` link reference** at the bottom of the README in the `<!-- LINKS -->` section:\n\n```markdown\n[migration_guide]: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/MigrationGuide.md\n```\n\n### Step 2: Add or update code snippets\n\nNo new snippet is required for the README changes described above. The existing `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/tests/samples/ReadmeSnippets.cs` snippets remain valid and unchanged.\n\n### Step 3: Verify README structure\n\nThe README at `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md` already contains all required sections in order:\n1. Getting started (Install the package, Prerequisites, Authenticate the client)\n2. Key concepts\n3. Examples\n4. Troubleshooting\n5. Next steps\n6. Contributing\n\nNo sections are missing; only the content within existing sections needs updating as described in Step 1.\n\n### Step 4: Validate\n\nRun these commands in order. Each must succeed before proceeding to the next. The snippet and API export scripts modify files in-place — those changes must be included in the commit.\n\n1. `dotnet build sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/`\n2. `dotnet test sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/ --filter TestCategory!=Live`\n3. `eng/scripts/Update-Snippets.ps1 identity` — confirm README snippet blocks are still injected correctly after edits; check git diff to verify\n4. `eng/scripts/Export-API.ps1 identity` — no public API changed, but run to confirm no regressions\n5. `dotnet format sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/`\n\n</details>\n\n## Next Steps\n\n> [!TIP]\n> **Ready for automated implementation?** Assign this issue to **@copilot** to have Copilot coding agent implement the changes described in the Implementation Guide above\n"}}

2026-04-09T21:08:24.2054329Z   
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2054858Z   This is incorrect now - the credentials no longer "live" in the `Azure.Identity` library, they live in `Azure.Core`. The line should be updated to reflect this.
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2055455Z   
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2055811Z   Now let me think about the most important snippet to add to the README. The key documentation needed is:
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2056480Z   1. In the "Key concepts" → "Credentials" section, add a note about the type consolidation
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2057298Z   2. In the "Getting started" → "Install the package" section, add a note that as of 1.53.0, Azure.Identity types are available directly from Azure.Core
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2057852Z   
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2057998Z   
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2058324Z   3. Include a link to the MigrationGuide.md for users who need guidance on updating their code
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2058723Z   
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2060409Z   I should draft a note that explains the consolidation clearly - starting with Azure.Core 1.53.0, all credential types are now compiled directly into Azure.Core, while Azure.Identity acts as a lightweight facade that type-forwards to it. This means projects using the newer version may not need a direct Azure.Identity reference anymore, and I should point users to the migration guide for more details.
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2062058Z

2026-04-09T21:08:24.2082869Z 
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2083327Z ✓ $ cat /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-net/azure-sdk-for-net/sdk/keyvault/Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets/README.md | head -30
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2083793Z 
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2084326Z ◆ I notice the KeyVault README has a migration guide link in the header. The Azure.Identity README doesn't have a link to MigrationGuide.md in its header.
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2084911Z   
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2085164Z   Now let me create the GitHub issue. The key gaps I've found are:
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2085473Z   
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2086512Z   1. **README.md - Getting Started section**: Doesn't mention that as of Azure.Core/Azure.Identity 1.53.0-beta.1, `Azure.Identity` is a lightweight facade - all credential types now live in `Azure.Core`. Users who only depend on `Azure.Core` 1.53.0+ can use all credential types without adding an `Azure.Identity` reference.
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2087614Z   
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2087761Z   
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2088550Z   2. **README.md - Key Concepts → Credentials section**: The description saying credentials are implementations of `TokenCredential` in Azure.Identity is outdated since they've moved to Azure.Core.
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2089965Z   3. **README.md - No link to MigrationGuide.md**: The migration guide addresses CS0433 errors users will hit during upgrades but isn't referenced anywhere, unlike the KeyVault README which includes it in the header.
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2090874Z   4. **README.md - Next steps section**: Missing a reference that should be there.
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2091226Z   
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2092290Z   The README is the first place users land when looking for Azure.Identity, yet it doesn't explain the architectural shift, doesn't mention they can use credentials from Azure.Core directly, and provides no guidance on CS0433 errors they might encounter. This is a significant gap in helping users through the upgrade path.
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2093360Z 
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2093482Z ◆ Now I'll create the issue:
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2093627Z 
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2094110Z ✓ $ cat /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-net/azure-sdk-for-net/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md | grep -n "\[migration" | head -5
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2094587Z 
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2094961Z ✓ $ sed -n '1,8p' /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-net/azure-sdk-for-net/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2095346Z 
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2096610Z ◆ One more important thing: the README description on line 3 says "It provides a set of `TokenCredential` implementations that can be used to construct Azure SDK clients that support Microsoft Entra token authentication." This is now stale - those implementations are in Azure.Core, not in Azure.Identity anymore.
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2097719Z   
2026-04-09T21:08:24.2098164Z   Also, the header links should include a migration guide link (like the KeyVault README has `[Migration guide][migration_guide]`).

CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 1 issue(s) can be created.","inputSchema":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{"body":{"description":"Detailed issue description in Markdown. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 42 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/42). Can also be a temporary_id (e.g., 'aw_abc123', 'aw_Test123') from a previously created issue in the same workflow run.","type":["number","string"]},"secrecy":{"description":"Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., \"public\", \"internal\", \"private\").","type":"string"},"temporary_id":{"description":"Unique temporary identifier for referencing this issue before it's created. Use '#aw_ID' in body text to reference other issues by their temporary_id; these are replaced with actual issue numbers after creation.","pattern":"^aw_[A-Za-z0-9]{3,12}$","type":"string"},"title":{"description":"Concise issue title summarizing the bug, feature, or task. Be specific about what functionality is needed.","type":"string"}},"required":["reason"],"type":"object"},"name":"missing_tool"},{"description":"Log a transparency message when no significant actions are needed. Use this to confirm workflow completion and provide visibility when analysis is complete but no changes or outputs are required (e.g., 'No issues found', 'All checks passed'). This ensures the workflow produces human-visible output even when no other actions are taken.","inputSchema":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{"integrity":{"description":"Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., \"low\", \"medium\", \"high\").","type":"string"},"message":{"description":"Status or completion message to log. Be specific about what data is needed.","type":"string"},"integrity":{"description":"Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., \"low\", \"medium\", \"high\").","type":"string"},"reason":{"description":"Explanation of why this data is needed to complete the task (max 256 characters).","type":"string"},"secrecy":{"description":"Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., \"public\", \"internal\", \"private\").","type":"string"}},"required":[],"type":"object"},"name":"missing_data"},{"description":"Signal that the task could not be completed due to an infrastructure or tool failure (e.g., MCP server crash, missing authentication, inaccessible repository). Use this when required tools or data are unavailable and the task cannot be meaningfully performed. This is distinct from noop (no action needed) — it indicates an active failure that prevented the task from running. The workflow framework will treat this as a failure signal even when the agent exits successfully.","inputSchema":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{"details":{"description":"Optional extended details or diagnostic context about the failure (max 65000 characters).","type":"string"},"reason":{"description":"A concise explanation of why the task could not be completed (max 1024 characters).
